user_request: Draft a suit for recovery of Rs.20,00,000/- paid as advance for a business transaction which failed due to Defendant's default. Plead total failure of consideration under Section 65 of Indian Contract Act. Claim refund with interest and costs. Include cause of action paragraph with accrual date, continuing nature of cause of action, valuation, court fee, and proper verification clause. Draft suitable for filing before the District Court.

facts.summary: Plaintiff seeks recovery of Rs. 20,00,000 paid as advance for a business transaction that failed due to the Defendant's default. Plaintiff claims total failure of consideration under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act and seeks refund with interest and costs.

slots.value: Recovery of advance with interest and costs

assumptions: Jurisdiction lies with the District Court based on the suit value or cause of action location
Section 65 applies as the consideration has wholly failed

must be read subject to its provisions. Under s 8 of Suits Valuation Act, 1887 the valuation for court fees and 
the valuation for jurisdiction in certain suits is the same. Under s 7 cl (iv)(f) of the Court Fees Act, 1870, the 
valuation for purposes of court fees in a suit for accounts lies in the discretion of the plaintiff. Therefore, in a suit 
for accounts, the plaintiffs valuation in the plaint fixes the jurisdiction of the court. 2 In a suit for unsettled 
accounts, fixed court fee is payable and for jurisdictional purposes, such a suit can be valued in terms of 
valuation as fixed by the plaintiff. 3 In a suit for partition, the value for the purpose of jurisdiction is the value of 
the share claimed by the plaintiff and not the value of the entire estate in which the partition is sought. 4 In a suit 
for recovery of property from the tenant whose tenancy has been terminated, the valuation of suit for 
jurisdictional purposes should be on the basis of annual rent or profit. Suit so valued along with the claim of 
damage exceeding pecuniary jurisdiction of the court is liable to be returned for prosecution before the proper 
court.5

it depend upon the character of the relief prayed for by the plaintiff. It refers to the media upon which the 
plaintiff asks the court to arrive at a conclusion in his favour.45
The failure to pay the loan instalments as per the terms of the agreement gives a cause of action to the bank to 
seek recovery of loan amount.46
4. Splitting of claim. Rules 1 and 2 are aimed against a multiplicity of suits in respect of the same cause of act 
ion.47 The object of r 2 is, to use the language of r 1 of this order, to prevent further litigation. For that purpose the 
rule provides that every suit shall include the whole of the claim which the plaintiff is entitled to make in respect of 
the same cause of action. He is not entitled to split his cause of act ion into parts, and bring separate suits in 
respect of each part. If he omits to sue in respect of, or intentionally relinquishes, any portion of the claim arising 
from the same cause of action, he will be precluded from suing in respect of the portion so omitted or relinquished, 
even though he states in his plaint that he intends to bring a

it facility and different accounts had been opened relating to one and the

plaint to include two more accounts. However, no new cause of act ion was introduced and no separate relief was 
claimed. It was held that since all accounts related to one and the same transaction and the amendment did not 
bring about any change in the nature and character of the suit, the amendment was fit to be allowed.28

sections.heading: BETWEEN
sections.instruction: Draft the parties section listing plaintiff and defendant(s) with full particulars: name, age, occupation, and address. Use sui juris averment for competency. If any detail is missing, use the appropriate {{PLACEHOLDER}}.

sections.heading: JURISDICTION
sections.instruction: Draft the jurisdiction section establishing: (1) territorial jurisdiction — defendant resides/works within court limits OR cause of action arose within limits, (2) pecuniary jurisdiction — claim amount falls within this court's monetary limits. State each ground as a separate numbered sub-paragraph.

sections.heading: FACTS OF THE CASE
sections.instruction: Draft the facts section as numbered paragraphs in chronological order. Cover: (1) relationship between parties, (2) the transaction/agreement, (3) payment details with amounts, (4) defendant's failure/default, (5) communications/notices between parties, (6) evidence references using Annexure labels. Use {{PLACEHOLDER}} for any missing facts. Reference documents as 'Annexure [LABEL]' per the annexure scheme.

sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
sections.instruction: Draft the legal basis section citing applicable statutory provisions from the verified provisions list. Structure as: (1) Primary ground with section citation and explanation, (2) Alternative plea (if applicable, e.g., Section 73 as alternative to Section 65 ICA), (3) How the facts satisfy each legal requirement. Cite ONLY provisions from the verified list. Do NOT invent case law citations.

must_include.description: At least one statutory section cited

sections.heading: CAUSE OF ACTION
sections.instruction: Draft the cause of action section stating: (1) when the cause of action first arose (with date or placeholder), (2) what acts/omissions constitute the cause, (3) where the cause arose (jurisdiction nexus), (4) the continuing nature of the cause of action (defendant's continued failure to pay). The cause must be anchored to the failure/default date, NOT the notice date.

sections.heading: LIMITATION
sections.instruction: Draft the limitation section stating: (1) the applicable Article of the Limitation Act, 1963 Schedule (from enrichment or use placeholder), (2) the limitation period, (3) the starting date of limitation (must be anchored to cause_of_action_date/default_date, NOT notice_date), (4) that the suit is within time. If limitation article is not available in enrichment, use {{LIMITATION_ARTICLE}} placeholder.

sections.heading: VALUATION AND COURT FEE
sections.instruction: Draft the valuation and court fee section stating: (1) suit valuation equals the claim amount, (2) court fee calculated per applicable state court fees act. Use court_fee context from web search if available, otherwise use {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} placeholder.

sections.heading: INTEREST
sections.instruction: Draft the interest section with a 3-part structure: (1) Pre-suit interest — from default date to filing date, (2) Pendente lite interest — during pendency of suit under Section 34 CPC, (3) Future interest — post-decree until realization. State the rate of interest (from intake or use {{INTEREST_RATE}} placeholder). Cite Section 34 of CPC for pendente lite interest.

sections.heading: PRAYER
sections.instruction: Draft the prayer section with at least 6 sub-prayers as lettered items (a, b, c, ...). Must include: (a) decree for principal amount, (b) pre-suit interest, (c) pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC, (d) future interest post-decree, (e) costs of the suit including pre-litigation costs, (f) any other relief the court deems fit. Each prayer must be specific with amounts or placeholders.

sections.heading: LIST OF DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON

Only list documents mentioned in evidence. If evidence is empty, list standard expected documents with {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}} placeholders.

filled_sections.text:
1. The Plaintiff and the Defendant are parties to a business arrangement evidenced by Annexure A (Agreement/Contract document), establishing a contractual relationship for the contemplated transaction.

2. The transaction/agreement between the Plaintiff and the Defendant was governed by the terms of the Agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} (Annexure A), under which the Plaintiff was to provide funds for the business transaction and the Defendant undertook to perform the corresponding obligations.

3. On {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}}, the Plaintiff paid Rs. 2,000,000 (Rupees Two Million) as an advance for the transaction, the payment evidenced by Annexure B (Payment proof). The Plaintiff seeks recovery of Rs. 20,00,000 (twenty lakh) paid as advance.

4. The Defendant defaulted on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} by not repaying or performing the agreed obligations, resulting in total failure of consideration. The Plaintiff pleads breach/failure of consideration under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act in relation to the Agreement (Annexure A) and the accompanying payment (Annexure B).

5. Communications and notices between the parties include the legal notice served by the Plaintiff on {{NOTICE_DATE}} (Annexure C). The Defendant replied on {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}} (Annexure D). If any acknowledgment/admission exists, it is referenced at Annexure E. Additional supporting documents, if any, are referenced at Annexure F.

6. The facts and the referenced documents collectively establish the Plaintiff’s claim for the recovery of the advance amount and related relief, with supporting evidence to be found in Annexures A, B, C, D, E, and F.

filled_sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
filled_sections.text:
Legal Basis and Grounds

1. Primary ground — Section 65 Indian Contract Act: Total failure of consideration and restitution
- The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, paid Rs. 20,00,000 as an advance under the agreement/contract with the Defendant, as evidenced by Annexure A (Agreement/Contract document) and Annexure B (Payment proof). The business transaction contemplated under the agreement failed due to the Defendant’s default on {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, giving rise to total failure of consideration.
- Under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, where there is total failure of consideration for a contract, the right to restitution attaches to the party who paid money. Consequently, the Plaintiff is entitled to recover the amount paid, i.e., Rs. 20,00,000, together with interest and costs as appropriate.
- The claim is framed within the applicable limitation regime, with accrual from the date of failure ({{DEFAULT_DATE}}) under Article 47 of the Limitation Act, and the suit to be filed within three years from that date (the filing date is {{FILING_DATE}}).

2. Alternative plea — Not applicable
- No alternate plea under a different statutory provision is invoked in the verified statutory provisions list. The primary ground under Section 65 ICA suffices to support restitution in the present facts.

3. How the facts satisfy the legal requirements
- Existence of a contract and payment: There was an agreement/contract between the Plaintiff and Defendant (Annexure A) and the Plaintiff made an advance payment of Rs. 20,00,000 (Annexure B).
- Cause of action arose from defendant’s default: The transaction failed due to the Defendant’s default on {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, resulting in a total failure of consideration.
- Legal basis for restitution: Section 65 ICA supports restitution where there is total failure of consideration; the Plaintiff’s relief is to recover the sum paid, together with interest and costs.
- Limitation considerations: Article 47 of the Limitation Act prescribes a three-year period from the date of failure for suits seeking money paid in light of a failed consideration, with accrual on {{DEFAULT_DATE}}; the present filing date is referenced as {{FILING_DATE}} to determine timeliness.
- Relief sought: Refund of Rs. 20,00,000 (principal amount paid) plus interest and costs; this aligns with the restitution principle under Section 65 ICA.

Documents relied upon and annexures:
- Annexure A: Agreement/Contract document
- Annexure B: Payment proof
- Annexure C: Legal notice (and Annexure D, if any, in response)

filled_sections.heading: CAUSE OF ACTION
filled_sections.text:
CAUSE OF ACTION

1. Accrual of the action
The cause of action first arose on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}}, being the date of the Defendant’s default in repaying/fulfilling its contractual obligations, specifically on {{DEFAULT_DATE}}. The Plaintiff had paid Rs. 2,000,000 as advance under the Agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} (Annexure A). The accrual is anchored to the date of default and not to any notice date.

2. Acts/omissions constituting the cause
The Plaintiff's claim is grounded on the Defendant’s failure to repay the advance and to perform the contract obligations, resulting in total failure of consideration under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872. By virtue of this default, the Plaintiff seeks recovery of the advance amount and the consequential relief as provided by law. The relevant documents establishing the basis of the claim include the Agreement (Annexure A) and the payment proof (Annexure B).

3. Jurisdiction/nexus (where the cause arose)
The cause of action arises within the jurisdiction corresponding to the place of formation/performance of the contract, as evidenced by the Agreement and related documents (Annexure A and Annexure B). The nexus with the appropriate jurisdiction is further supported by the location/place_names pertinent to the Parties and the contract.

4. Continuing nature of the cause
This is a continuing cause of action, since the Defendant’s ongoing failure to refund the advance amount constitutes an ongoing breach of the contract. The liability to repay remains unresolved until the full amount of Rs. 2,000,000 is returned to the Plaintiff; each day of continued non-payment reinforces the Plaintiff’s claim.

5. Reference to statutory basis
The claim is framed under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, on account of total failure of consideration where an advance payment has been made and the promisor fails to perform.

Annexures relied upon: Annexure A (Agreement/Contract document), Annexure B (Payment proof), Annexure C (Legal notice) to support the above cause of action.

- Period: Three years.

- Within limitation: The suit is within time, as it is brought within three years from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} (the date of failure) and anchored to the cause_of_action_date {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}}. The filing date {{FILING_DATE}} also lies within this three-year window.

3. The Plaintiff and the Defendant are parties to a business arrangement evidenced by Annexure A (Agreement/Contract document), establishing a contractual relationship for the contemplated transaction.

- Under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, where there is total failure of consideration for a contract, the right to restitution attaches to the party who paid money. Consequently, the Plaintiff is entitled to recover the amount paid, i.e., Rs. 20,00,000, together with interest and costs as appropriate.
- The claim is framed within the applicable limitation regime, with accrual from the date of failure ({{DEFAULT_DATE}}) under Article 47 of the Limitation Act, and the suit to be filed within three years from that date (the filing date is {{FILING_DATE}}).

This is a continuing cause of action, since the Defendant’s ongoing failure to refund the advance amount constitutes an ongoing breach of the contract. 2. Pecuniary jurisdiction: The suit claim is for recovery of Rs. 20,00,000/- together with interest and costs, which falls within the pecuniary jurisdiction of this Hon’ble District Court at {{COURT_PLACE}}, {{STATE}}.


FACTS OF THE CASE

3. The Plaintiff and the Defendant entered into a business arrangement evidenced by an Agreement/Contract dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} (Annexure A).

4. Pursuant to the said Agreement/Contract, the Plaintiff was required to pay an advance towards the contemplated business transaction, and the Defendant undertook to perform the corresponding obligations as per the agreed terms.

5. On {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}}, the Plaintiff paid a sum of Rs. 20,00,000/- as advance to the Defendant towards the said transaction. The said payment is evidenced by the payment proof (Annexure B).

6. The Defendant committed default on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} by failing to perform the agreed obligations and/or failing to refund the advance, resulting in total failure of consideration. The Plaintiff pleads total failure of consideration and restitution under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872.

7. The Plaintiff issued a legal notice to the Defendant on {{NOTICE_DATE}} calling upon the Defendant to refund the advance amount (Annexure C). The Defendant replied on {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}} (Annexure D) (if any).

8. Despite the above, the Defendant has neither performed the transaction nor refunded the advance amount of Rs. 20,00,000/-. Hence, this suit.


LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS

9. Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 — Total failure of consideration and restitution

(a) The Plaintiff paid Rs. 20,00,000/- as advance under the Agreement/Contract (Annexure A), as evidenced by payment proof (Annexure B).

(b) The business transaction contemplated under the Agreement failed due to the Defendant’s default on {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, resulting in total failure of consideration.

(c) Under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, when an agreement becomes void / consideration fails, the party who has received any advantage is bound to restore it or make compensation to the other party. Accordingly, the Defendant is liable to refund the advance amount of Rs. 20,00,000/- to the Plaintiff, together with interest and costs.

(d) The Plaintiff’s claim is within limitation as pleaded below.


CAUSE OF ACTION

10. Accrual of cause of action: The cause of action first arose on {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, being the date of the Defendant’s default in performing the obligations and/or refunding the advance amount, resulting in total failure of consideration.

11. Acts/omissions constituting the cause of action: The Defendant’s failure to perform the transaction and failure to refund the advance amount of Rs. 20,00,000/-, despite demand, gives rise to the Plaintiff’s right to sue for refund/restitution under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872.

12. Place where cause of action arose: The cause of action arose within the territorial jurisdiction of this Hon’ble Court inasmuch as the transaction/agreement and related acts (including payment and obligations to be performed) pertain to {{COURT_PLACE}}, {{STATE}}, as evidenced by Annexure A and Annexure B.

13. Continuing/ subsisting nature: The Defendant’s liability to refund the advance remains subsisting so long as the Defendant continues to withhold the sum of Rs. 20,00,000/-; however, the accrual date and limitation are pleaded from the date of failure/default as stated herein.


LIMITATION

14. The suit is governed by Article 47 of the Limitation Act, 1963 (For money paid upon an existing consideration which afterwards fails), which prescribes a period of three years from the date of the failure.

15. In the present case, the failure/default occurred on {{DEFAULT_DATE}}. The present suit is filed on {{FILING_DATE}} and is within the period of limitation.


VALUATION AND COURT FEE

16. Valuation: For the purposes of court fee and jurisdiction, the suit is valued at Rs. 20,00,000/- being the principal amount claimed.

17. Court fee: The requisite court fee is payable as per the applicable State Court Fees Act on the above valuation. The court fee amount is {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}.


INTEREST

18. The Plaintiff claims interest as follows:

(a) Pre-suit interest: On Rs. 20,00,000/- at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}}.

(b) Pendente lite interest: Under Section 34 CPC, interest on the decreetal amount at such rate as this Hon’ble Court may deem fit from the date of institution of the suit ({{FILING_DATE}}) till the date of decree.

(c) Future interest: Interest on the decretal amount at such rate as this Hon’ble Court may deem fit from the date of decree till realization.


PRAYER

The Plaintiff therefore prays that this Hon’ble Court may be pleased to:

a) Pass a decree directing the Defendant to pay the Plaintiff a sum of Rs. 20,00,000/- (Rupees Twenty Lakh Only) being refund/restitution of the advance amount under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872;

b) Grant pre-suit interest on Rs. 20,00,000/- at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}}, amounting to Rs. {{PRE_SUIT_INTEREST_AMOUNT}}/-;

c) Grant pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC on the decreetal amount from the date of institution of the suit ({{FILING_DATE}}) till the date of decree, amounting to Rs. {{PENDENTE_LITE_AMOUNT}}/- (as may be computed);

d) Grant future interest on the decretal amount from the date of decree till realization, amounting to Rs. {{POST_DECREE_INTEREST_AMOUNT}}/- (as may be computed);

e) Award costs of the suit; and

f) Grant such other and further reliefs as this Hon’ble Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.

2. Annexure B: Proof of payment of advance of Rs. 20,00,000/- dated {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}}.

(a) The Plaintiff paid Rs. 20,00,000/- as advance under the Agreement/Contract (Annexure A), as evidenced by payment proof (Annexure B).

(b) The business transaction contemplated under the Agreement failed due to the Defendant’s default on {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, resulting in total failure of consideration.

(c) Under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, when an agreement becomes void / consideration fails, the party who has received any advantage is bound to restore it or make compensation to the other party. Accordingly, the Defendant is liable to refund the advance amount of Rs. 20,00,000/- to the Plaintiff, together with interest and costs.

(d) The Plaintiff’s claim is within limitation as pleaded below.


CAUSE OF ACTION

10. Accrual of cause of action: The cause of action first arose on {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, being the date of the Defendant’s default in performing the obligations and/or refunding the advance amount, resulting in total failure of consideration.

11. Acts/omissions constituting the cause of action: The Defendant’s failure to perform the transaction and failure to refund the advance amount of Rs. 20,00,000/-, despite demand, gives rise to the Plaintiff’s right to sue for refund/restitution under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872.

12. Place where cause of action arose: The cause of action arose within the territorial jurisdiction of this Hon’ble Court inasmuch as the transaction/agreement and related acts (including payment and obligations to be performed) pertain to {{COURT_PLACE}}, {{STATE}}, as evidenced by Annexure A and Annexure B.

13. Continuing/ subsisting nature: The Defendant’s liability to refund the advance remains subsisting so long as the Defendant continues to withhold the sum of Rs. 20,00,000/-; however, the accrual date and limitation are pleaded from the date of failure/default as stated herein.


LIMITATION

14. The suit is governed by Article 47 of the Limitation Act, 1963 (For money paid upon an existing consideration which afterwards fails), which prescribes a period of three years from the date of the failure.

15. In the present case, the failure/default occurred on {{DEFAULT_DATE}}. The present suit is filed on {{FILING_DATE}} and is within the period of limitation.


VALUATION AND COURT FEE

16. Valuation: For the purposes of court fee and jurisdiction, the suit is valued at Rs. 20,00,000/- being the principal amount claimed.

17. Court fee: The requisite court fee is payable as per the applicable State Court Fees Act on the above valuation. The court fee amount is {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}.


INTEREST

18. The Plaintiff claims interest as follows:

(a) Pre-suit interest: On Rs. 20,00,000/- at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}}.

(b) Pendente lite interest: Under Section 34 CPC, interest on the decreetal amount at such rate as this Hon’ble Court may deem fit from the date of institution of the suit ({{FILING_DATE}}) till the date of decree.

(c) Future interest: Interest on the decretal amount at such rate as this Hon’ble Court may deem fit from the date of decree till realization.


PRAYER

The Plaintiff therefore prays that this Hon’ble Court may be pleased to:

a) Pass a decree directing the Defendant to pay the Plaintiff a sum of Rs. 20,00,000/- (Rupees Twenty Lakh Only) being refund/restitution of the advance amount under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872;

b) Grant pre-suit interest on Rs. 20,00,000/- at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}}, amounting to Rs. {{PRE_SUIT_INTEREST_AMOUNT}}/-;

c) Grant pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC on the decreetal amount from the date of institution of the suit ({{FILING_DATE}}) till the date of decree, amounting to Rs. {{PENDENTE_LITE_AMOUNT}}/- (as may be computed);

d) Grant future interest on the decretal amount from the date of decree till realization, amounting to Rs. {{POST_DECREE_INTEREST_AMOUNT}}/- (as may be computed);

e) Award costs of the suit; and

f) Grant such other and further reliefs as this Hon’ble Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.
